                                        U.S. DEPARTMENT OF LABOR
                                                 Ray Marshall, Secretary


                                            BUREAU OF LABOR STATISTICS
                                              Julius Shiskin, Commissioner


                                        Employment and Earnings is prepared by the
                                    Division of Industry Employment Statistics and the
                                    Division of Employment and Unemployment Anal-
                                    ysis in collaboration with the Division of Special
                                    Publications. The data are collected by the Bureau
                                    of the Census (Department of Commerce), Employ-
                                    ment and Training Administration, State Employ-
                                    ment Security Agencies, and State Departments of
                                    Labor in cooperation with the Bureau of Labor
                                    Statistics. A brief description of the cooperative
                                    statistical programs of the BLS with these agencies
                                    is presented in the Explanatory Notes. The State
                                    agencies are listed on the inside back cover.
                                         Employment and Earnings may be ordered
                                    through the Superintendent of Documents, U.S.
                                    Government Printing Office, Washington, D.C
                                    20402. Subscription price per year $18 domes-
                                    tic, and $22.50 foreign. Single copy $1.50 domes-
                                    tic,and $1.90 foreign. Prices are subject to change
                                    by the U.S. Government Printing Office. Send
                                    correspondence on circulation and subscription
                                    matters (including address changes) to the Super-
                                    intendent of Documents.

                                        Communications on editorial matters should be
                                    addressed to: Editors, Employment and Earnings,
                                    Bureau of Labor Statistics, Washington, D.C. 20212.
                                    Inquiries regarding the text and Household Data
                                    should be addressed to: Attention of Gloria P.
                                    Green or Phone: (202) 523-1944. Inquiries relating
                                    to Establishment Data and all other tables should be
                                    addressed to: Attention of Chester L. Green or
                                    Phone: (202) 523-1759.

                                        The Secretary of Labor has determined that
                                    the publication of this periodical is necessary in
                                    the transaction of the public business required by
                                    law of this Department. Use of funds for printing
                                    this periodical has been approved by the Director
                                    of the Office of Management and Budget through
                                    February 28, 1981. Controlled circulation postage
                                    paid at Washington, D.C. and Baltimore, Md.

                                    Library of Congress Catalog Number 70-11379

                                        Unless identified as copyrighted, articles and
                                    tabulations in this publication are in the public
                                    domain and may be reproduced without permis-
                                    sion of the Federal Government. Please credit Em-
                                    ployment and Earnings, Bureau of Labor Statistics.

               which was signed into law on May 23, 1977, provides sub-        (or taxable income credit) for all workers and the earned

                                           Labor force and unemployment estimates for counties, cities, and other small areas have been prepared for administration of various
                                        Federal economic assistance programs and may be ordered from the National Technical Information Service, U.S. Department of Commerce,
                                        5285 Port Royal Road, Springfield, Virginia 22161. When ordering please, specify "CETA Area Employment and Unemployment, January-
                                        April 1977 (NTISUB/C/143-77/006), "States and County Employment and Unemployment January-April 1977" {NTISUB/C/144-77/006)
                                        or "Unemployment Rates for States and Local Governments, First Quarter. 1977" (PB267950). Tabulations are available at $8.00 per set for
                                        printed copies of CETA Areas, $13.75 for States and Counties, $6.00 for States and Local Governments, or $3.00 per set for microfiche
                                        copies of each publication.

                                                                      Introduction
                The statistics in this periodical are compiled from two            rived only from establishment reports.
              major sources: (1) Household interviews, and (2) reports from            Data from these two sources differ from each other because
              employers,                                                           of differences in definition and coverage, sources of informa-
                                                                                   tion, methods of collection, and estimating procedures. Sam-
                                                                                   pling variability and response errors are additional reasons for
                                                                                   discrepancies. The major factors which have a differential
                  Data based on household interviews are obtained from a           effect on levels and trends of the two series are as follows.
              sample survey of the population 16 years of age and over. The
              survey is conducted each month by the Bureau of the Census
              for the Bureau of Labor Statistics and provides comprehensive        Employment
              data on the labor force, the employed and the unemployed,
              including such characteristics as age, sex, race, household          Coverage. The household survey definition of employment
              relationship, marital status, occupation, and industry attach-       comprises wage and salary workers (including domestics and
              ment. The survey also provides data on the characteristics and       other private household workers), self-employed persons, and
              past work experience of those not in the labor force. The infor-     unpaid workers who worked 15 hours or more during the sur-
              mation is collected by trained interviewers from a sample of         vey week in family-operated enterprises. Employment in both
              about 47,000 households, representing 461 areas in 923               agricultural and nonagricultural industries is included. The
              counties and independent cities, with coverage in 50 States          payroll survey covers only wage and salary employees on the
              and the District of Columbia. The data collected are based on        payrolls of nonagricultural establishments.
              the activity or status reported for the calendar week including
              the 12th of the month.                                               Multiple jobholding. The household approach provides infor-
                                                                                   mation on the work status of the population without duplication,
                                                                                   since each person is classified as employed, unemployed, or
                                                                                   not in the labor force. Employed persons holding more than
                                                                                   one job are counted only once and are classified according to
                  Data based on establishment records are compiled each            the job at which they worked the greatest number of hours
              month from mail questionnaires by the Bureau of Labor Statis-        during the survey week. In the figures based on establishment
              tics, in cooperation with State agencies. The establishment          records, persons who worked in more than one establishment
              survey is designed to provide detailed industry information          during the reporting period are counted each time their names
              on nonagricultural wage and salary employment, average               appear on payrolls.
              weekly hours, average hourly and weekly earnings, and labor
              turnover for the Nation, States, and metropolitan areas. The         Unpaid absences from jobs. The household survey includes
              employment, hours, and earnings series are based on payroll
                                                                                   among the employed all persons who had jobs but were not at
              reports from a sample of establishments employing over 30
                                                                                   work during the survey week—that is, were not working but
              million nonagricultural wage and salary workers. The data
                                                                                   had jobs from which they were temporarily absent because of
              relate to all workers, full- or part-time, who received pay during
                                                                                   illness, bad weather, vacation*; labor-management dispute, or
              the payroll period which includes the 12th of the month. Based
                                                                                   because they were taking time off for various other reasons,
              on a somewhat smaller sample, labor turnover data relate to

              actions occurring during the entire month.
                                                                                   In the figures based on payroll reports, persons on leave paid
                                                                                   for by the company are included, but not those on leave with-
                                                                                   out pay for the entire payroll period.

                                                                                   household and establishment survey employment data, see

                                                                           Establishment data
                                                                            (B, C, and D tables)


               COLLECTION                                                                        State agencies mail the forms to the establishments and
                                                                                              examine the returns for consistency, accuracy, and complete-
                  Payroll reports provide current information on wage and                     ness. The States use the information to prepare State and area
               salary employment, hours, earnings, and labor turnover in non-                 series and then send the establishment data to the BLS for use
               agricultural establishments, by industry and geographic location.              in preparing the national series.


               Federal-State cooperation                                                      Shuttle schedules

                   Under cooperative arrangements with State agencies, the                        Two types of data collection schedules are used: Form BLS
               respondent fills out a single employment or labor turnover                     790—Monthly Report on Employment, Payroll, and Hours; and
               reporting form, which is then used for national, State, and area               Form DL 1219—Monthly Report on Labor Turnover. These
               estimates. This eliminates duplicate reporting on the part of                  schedules are of the "shuttle" type, with space for each month
               respondents, and together with the use of identical techniques                 of the calendar year. The collection agency returns the schedule
               at the national and State levels, insures maximum comparability                to the respondent each month so that the next month's data
               of estimates.                                                                  can be entered. This procedure assures maximum comparability

               Labor turnover
                                                                                        From a sample composed of establishments reporting for
                   Labor turnover is the gross movement of wage and salary          both the previous and current months, the ratio of current
               workers into and out of employed status with respect to indi-        month employment to that of the previous month is computed.
               vidual establishments. This movement, which relates to a cal-        This is called a link relative. The estimates of employment (all

               initiated by either employer or employee). Each type of action       estimates for the previous month by these "link relatives." In
